title: The Mill Where Language First Divides

features.name: the-river-and-saw
features.desc-on-touch: Water and blade meet at the same point—the river powers the mill, and the mill cuts the thing the river brings. Here intention becomes object: tree-as-material enters the mill, exits as fence-post-as-boundary.

features.name: the-timber-stacks
features.desc-on-touch: Logs are piled by grade, dimension, and intended use. But an oral narrative is carved into each stack's label—a cordel verse, a folk tale about a wanderer—and the archive cannot decide if the story marks the wood or the wood marks the story.

features.name: the-mill-worker-s-glossary
features.desc-on-touch: A hanging board with terms: the oral project documented how the same wooden piece carries different names in different stories, each name correct in its own narrative world.

features.name: the-first-division
features.desc-on-touch: A crack runs through the mill floor—the line between what arrives and what departs. On one side, the forest; on the other, the built world. Language separates here into wood-language and story-language, and they have never met again.

inscription: this is the place where the tree agreed to stop being alive and the fence agreed to start being a boundary

file.name: Mill_Operations_Log_1973_West_African_Cordel_Variants_Appended.txt
file.note: ERROR: narrative structure and operational history have become indistinguishable; the mill is told in multiple simultaneous folk traditions

exits.label: back down-stream to the maintenance depot, where the posts remember their timber mill beginning
further upstream toward the forest where timber was still part of a tree
toward the archive's deep-patience station, where long work is recorded
